text: Create a server component that displays the current server timestamp on each request. The timestamp should be different on every page load (not prerendered at build time). Use Next.js's recommended approach for opting out of static prerendering when you need dynamic data without using cookies/headers.

text: The app is a minimal App Router project. I’ll add a dedicated server component using `connection()` from `next/server`, which is Next.js’s request-time opt-out for dynamic data that does not depend on request APIs, and replace the placeholder random-number content with the timestamp display.

The component is now request-bound through `await connection()` and computes the ISO timestamp after that boundary. I’m running the TypeScript check and production build next to catch any version-specific API or rendering issues.

input.command:
/bin/bash -lc 'first=$(curl -fsS http://localhost:3000/ | sed -n '"'s/.*<time dateTime=\"\\(["'^"]*'"\\)\".*/\\1/p')
second="'$(curl -fsS http://localhost:3000/ | sed -n '"'s/.*<time dateTime=\"\\(["'^"]*'"\\)\".*/\\1/p')
printf 'first=%s\\nsecond=%s\\n' \""'$first" "$second"
test -n "$first" && test -n "$second" && test "$first" != "$second"'

text:
Implemented the request-time server timestamp.

- Added [`app/server-timestamp.tsx`](/home/user/workspace/app/server-timestamp.tsx) using `await connection()` and `new Date().toISOString()`.
- Updated [`app/page.tsx`](/home/user/workspace/app/page.tsx) to render it.
- Verified `/` is dynamic in the production build.
- Confirmed two successive requests return different timestamps.
- TypeScript check passes.
